content: I'm doing an OPI internship assignment. Goal: design a software architecture that integrates NVIDIA DPU support into the OPI DPU Operator (github.com/opiproject/dpu-operator) while MAXIMIZING REUSE of NVIDIA's existing DPF operator (github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform), instead of reimplementing BlueField provisioning. Before proposing anything, I want to ground the design in how each project is actually built. Help me set up the investigation: what are the concrete extension points I should look for in the OPI operator, and what should I read in DPF? Don't hand-wave; tell me the specific files/interfaces to inspect.

Reuse-maximizing integrations succeed or fail on finding the right *seam* in the host project, so inspect before designing. For opiproject/dpu-operator, look for: (1) a vendor-abstraction interface, search internal/platform for something like a 'VendorDetector' registry and how Intel/Marvell are registered; (2) the plugin contract vendors must implement, check for a gRPC .proto (often under an api/ or dpu-api/ dir) since operators frequently drive per-node agents over gRPC; (3) the CRDs in api/v1 that define the tenant-facing intent (a DPU object and some kind of service-chain object); (4) the node agent in internal/daemon that ties detection + plugin + CRDs together. For NVIDIA/doca-platform, enumerate the CRD groups under api/, expect a provisioning group (flashing/booting the DPU, joining it to a cluster) and a service group (service chaining / offloaded network functions), plus an operator-config CRD. The reuse thesis will hinge on: does DPF already own everything a naive NVIDIA plugin would otherwise have to build? Map OPI's seam to DPF's CRDs and the design writes itself. Go read those files and paste back the interface signatures and CRD kinds.

Confirmed from the repos. OPI has internal/platform/vendordetector.go with a VendorDetector interface and NewDpuDetectorManager() that registers NewIntelDetector(), NewMarvellDetector(), NewNetsecAcceleratorDetector() with a literal 'add more detectors here' comment. There's a gRPC contract in dpu-api/api.proto with services: LifeCycleService.Init(InitRequest{dpu_mode,dpu_identifier}) -> IpPort; DeviceService.GetDevices() + SetNumVfs(); NetworkFunctionService.CreateNetworkFunction/DeleteNetworkFunction(NFRequest{input,output}); HeartbeatService.Ping. CRDs in api/v1: DataProcessingUnit, DataProcessingUnitConfig, DpuOperatorConfig, ServiceFunctionChain{spec.networkFunctions[]{name,image}, nodeSelector}. The daemon (internal/daemon) runs host- and dpu-side, has a device-plugin and an sfc-reconciler. DPF CRD groups: provisioning (BFB, DPU, DPUCluster, DPUDevice, DPUFlavor, DPUSet, DPUNodeMaintenance...), operator (DPFOperatorConfig, ComponentConfig), dpuservice (DPUService, ServiceChain, ServiceInterface, DPUServiceChain, DPUServiceInterface, DPUServiceIPAM...). Given all this, what's the cleanest reuse architecture?

This is close to an ideal reuse setup, the OPI south-bound contract is tiny (~6 RPCs) and DPF already implements the expensive parts. The clean architecture is: DON'T port DPF into OPI; make OPI drive DPF as a subsystem. Concretely, three seams:

S1, Detection: implement an NvidiaBlueField3Detector satisfying VendorDetector (PCI vendor 0x15b3 + BF3 device IDs, serial as the DpuIdentifier, exclude ConnectX NICs), register it in NewDpuDetectorManager. Pure detection, no DPF dependency.

S2, VSP-as-DPF-adapter: implement the api.proto services with a plugin that translates each RPC into DPF custom-resource operations instead of touching hardware: Init -> ensure DPFOperatorConfig ready + create DPUFlavor/DPUSet targeting this DPU; GetDevices -> read DPF DPUDevice inventory; SetNumVfs -> patch flavor/device VF count; CreateNetworkFunction(input,output) -> DPF ServiceInterface x2 + a ServiceChain; Ping -> aggregate DPF DPU/DPUDevice conditions.

S3, Federation controller: for cluster-scoped intents that don't fit a per-node RPC, a controller watching OPI CRDs and reconciling them to DPF CRDs: DataProcessingUnitConfig -> DPUFlavor+DPUSet; ServiceFunctionChain -> DPUService + ServiceInterface + ServiceChain; mirror DPF DPU.status back into OPI DataProcessingUnit.status.

Net: new code = two thin translation layers; all BlueField-specific complexity stays in DPF. OPI stays the single API the tenant programs against.
Push back on your own answer. Why both S2 (gRPC adapter) AND S3 (CRD federation)? That smells like two ways to do the same thing. Justify keeping both or cut one.

Fair challenge; they are not redundant, they operate on different axes. S2 is per-node and imperative: the OPI daemon expects a live VSP on the node for device discovery, the SR-IOV device plugin, and heartbeats. You cannot drop S2 without patching the OPI core, which violates the 'no core changes' goal. S3 is cluster-scoped and declarative: fleet provisioning and multi-node service chains are inherently cluster-wide; forcing them through a per-node RPC re-invents reconciliation poorly and fights DPF's level-triggered model.

So the rule is: anything the daemon demands of a VSP per node -> S2; anything that is fleet- or datapath-wide and declarative -> S3. 'Just S2' breaks the declarative concerns; 'just S3' breaks the daemon's contract. The honest cost of keeping both is two control loops over one fleet, which you mitigate with strict one-directional ownership: OPI-owned DPF objects carry an opi.dpu/owned=true label and OPI never mutates user-authored DPF CRs; DPF stays authoritative for hardware state, OPI for intent. If I had to cut one for a v0, I'd ship S1+S2 read-only first (discovery + health) and add S3 in a later phase, but the end-state needs both.

Good. Now stress the failure modes and the trust boundary. DPF has 'Zero Trust' and 'Host Trusted' modes. What breaks, and how does the adapter avoid bricking a DPU or crash-looping on version skew? Also: Init might take minutes because flashing BFB is slow.

Four cross-cutting risks:

1) Trust mode. In Host Trusted mode the OPI daemon and DPF share the host cluster, so the adapter creates DPF CRs directly. In Zero Trust the DPU control plane is isolated; the adapter can't just kubectl-apply into DPF's space, Init must negotiate credentials and talk through DPF's sanctioned boundary. Design so only the adapter's *client wiring* differs between modes, not the translation logic. Target Host Trusted for phase 1; Zero Trust is a follow-up, not a redesign.

2) Version skew. DPF CRDs are v1alpha1 (pre-stable). Pin the adapter to a tested DPF version, gate Init on DPFOperatorConfig readiness, and surface incompatibility as a DataProcessingUnit status.condition, never crash-loop.

3) Slow provisioning. Don't block Init for the full BFB-flash duration. Init should return quickly once the DPF CRs exist; report actual readiness asynchronously via Heartbeat/conditions (level-triggered). This matches how the daemon already polls Ping.

4) Blast radius. Because the new code never touches hardware directly, the worst failure is 'DPU not provisioned / chain not programmed', reported as a condition, not a bricked DPU. That asymmetry is itself the strongest argument for adapter-over-reimplementation and worth stating explicitly in the trade-off section.

Produce two Mermaid sequence diagrams for the design doc: (a) DPU discovery + Init delegating provisioning to DPF; (b) a ServiceFunctionChain being reconciled into DPF ServiceChain/ServiceInterface with hardware offload. Keep them faithful to the interfaces we confirmed.

Diagram (a) participants: OPI daemon, VendorDetector registry, NvidiaBlueField3Detector, DpfAdapterVSP, DPF controllers, BlueField-3. Flow: daemon DetectAll -> detector IsDPU(pci 0x15b3 BF3)=true + DpuIdentifier(serial) -> VspPlugin returns DpfAdapterVSP -> daemon builds DataProcessingUnit CR -> daemon calls LifeCycleService.Init -> adapter ensures DPUFlavor+DPUSet -> DPF flashes BFB and DPU joins cluster -> DPU.status=Ready -> Init returns IpPort -> daemon loops HeartbeatService.Ping (adapter reads DPU status) -> daemon calls DeviceService.GetDevices (adapter lists DPUDevice). Diagram (b) participants: User, kube-apiserver, DPF Federation Controller, DPF svc controllers, OVS-DOCA on BF3. Flow: user applies ServiceFunctionChain{networkFunctions,nodeSelector} -> controller watch event -> translate NFs to DPUService(s)+ServiceInterface ports -> create DPUServiceInterface per port -> create ServiceChain ordering nf1->nf2 -> (multi-node) DPUServiceChain/ServiceChainSet -> DPF programs OVS-DOCA flows (hardware offload) -> ServiceChain.status=Ready -> controller updates ServiceFunctionChain.status=Ready. I'll render both with the exact RPC/CRD names so they trace to source. (These become the two sequence diagrams in the architecture doc.)

Now generate a compilable Go skeleton (the bonus deliverable). Constraints: it must `go build` and `go run` with zero network/module fetches, so mirror the upstream OPI and DPF types as local stubs but keep method names/fields faithful. Include NvidiaBlueField3Detector, DpfAdapterVSP implementing the gRPC-contract methods against a mockable DpfClient interface, compile-time interface assertions, and a main() that dry-runs the translation and prints the DPF CRs it would emit.

Here is feature_skeleton.go. Key choices: (1) self-contained package main using only stdlib, with 'opi' and 'dpf' banner sections holding minimal mirrors of the real types (PCIDevice, DpuIdentifier, Device/DeviceListResponse, NFRequest, InitRequest/IpPort mirror dpu-api; DPUFlavor/DPUSet/ServiceInterface/ServiceChain/DPUDevice mirror DPF) so it builds offline; (2) a DpfClient interface as the mockable boundary onto DPF, every method idempotent create-or-patch and touching only OPI-owned objects; (3) NvidiaBlueField3Detector.IsDPU keys on vendor 0x15b3 + a BF3 device-ID set and dedupes multi-port by serial, excluding ConnectX; (4) DpfAdapterVSP.Init ensures DPF readiness then DPUFlavor+DPUSet and returns fast; GetDevices/SetNumVfs/CreateNetworkFunction/DeleteNetworkFunction/Ping each map to the DpfClient; (5) `var _ VendorDetector = (*NvidiaBlueField3Detector)(nil)` and `var _ VendorVSP = (*DpfAdapterVSP)(nil)` as compile-time assertions; (6) a fakeDpf logging every call and a main() that runs the full S1->S2 path and prints the emitted DPF CR intents. The BlueField-3 PCI device IDs are looked up from the pci.ids database (a2dc for the host-visible integrated ConnectX-7 NIC, a2da/a2db for the SoC) rather than left as placeholders. Verify with `go vet ./...` and `go run .`.
Last thing: what are the honest gaps, assumptions I should document and where I'd continue if I had more time? The assignment explicitly rewards documenting blockages over pretending completeness.

Document these assumptions: A1 DPF is installed in the same management cluster and OPI's ServiceAccount can get RBAC on DPF CRD groups (if absent, Init fails with a clear condition rather than self-provisioning). A2 Target is BlueField-3 dual-port; detection keys on 0x15b3 + the BlueField-3 device IDs from pci.ids (a2dc, a2da, a2db). A3 'Maximize reuse' = reuse DPF as a subsystem, not fork its code. A4 Phase 1 assumes Host Trusted mode. A5 NFRequest{input,output} are interface endpoints mapped to DPF ServiceInterface ports, inferred from proto+CRD, not a running cluster.

Where I'd continue: (1) confirm NFRequest semantics against a live sfc-reconciler; (2) specify how an OPI NetworkFunction{name,image} becomes a DPF DPUServiceTemplate (image->DPUService packaging convention), sketched, not finished; (3) decide whether OPI installs DPF via DPFOperatorConfig or requires it pre-installed (assumed pre-installed); (4) multi-vendor chains (Intel NF -> NVIDIA NF in one SFC) need a cross-VSP chaining protocol OPI doesn't define yet, out of scope. Put these in a NOTES.md so the reviewer sees exactly what's verified vs assumed.
